ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for shioyaki salmon set meal. Mapped to closest parent salmon food product term. The set meal composite (rice, miso soup, salmon) lacks a direct FoodOn entry.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a composite teishoku set meal code. A00GJ (salmon, cooked) used as the closest parent anchor. Rice and miso soup components are not separately coded here.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT clinical food concept found for shioyaki salmon set meal as a composite dish.

main_ingredients: miso soup (miso paste, dashi, tofu, wakame seaweed)
allergens: gluten (miso paste, soy sauce, tempura batter)
shellfish (dashi may contain dried shrimp or bonito)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard shioyaki grilled salmon set meal weighing approximately 600 g, comprising a salt-grilled salmon fillet (~150 g), steamed white rice (~200 g), miso soup (~200 ml), and optional side dishes such as tempura or pickled vegetables (~50 g).

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Salmon, Atlantic, farmed, cooked, dry heat (FDC ID 175168) combined with cooked white rice (FDC ID 169756) and miso soup components, weighted by estimated set meal composition
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite shioyaki salmon teishoku set meal. Nutrient values per 100 g are estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central values for: (1) Atlantic salmon, farmed, cooked dry heat (~25% of set by weight, ~150 g); (2) cooked white rice, unenriched (~33% of set, ~200 g); (3) miso soup with tofu and wakame (~33% of set, ~200 g); (4) optional tempura/pickled vegetables (~8%, ~50 g). The input record's per-100g values (energy 125 kcal, protein 9.17 g, fat 5.83 g, carbohydrate 9.17 g) are broadly consistent with this weighted composite and are retained with medium confidence. Sodium at 300 mg/100 g is plausible given miso paste and salt-grilling contributions. All values cross-checked against USDA FDC component entries.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; Diabetes Care 2008;31(12):2281-2283; white rice GI = 64 (University of Sydney GI Database reference food)
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI value exists for a composite shioyaki salmon teishoku set meal. The GI of the set meal is dominated by the steamed white rice component (~33% by weight, ~200 g of 600 g total). White rice (GI = 64, medium) is used as the primary GI anchor per the University of Sydney GI Database. The salmon, miso soup, and vegetable components contribute negligible carbohydrates and do not materially alter the composite GI. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GL = (GI × available carbohydrate per serving in grams) / 100 = (64 × 55.0) / 100 = 35.2, rounded to 35.3. Available carbohydrate per 600 g serving = 9.17 g/100 g × 600 g = 55.0 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 300 mg/100 g; estimated 1800 mg per 600 g set meal — approaching 78% of the WHO daily sodium limit of 2300 mg in a single meal
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request reduced miso paste, minimal soy sauce, and reduced salt during grilling to lower the sodium content of this set meal, and avoid adding soy sauce at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 9.17 g/100 g (55 g per serving) primarily from white rice (GI 64, medium); protein 9.17 g/100 g from salmon supports glycaemic stability
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with type 2 diabetes should request brown rice or a smaller rice portion to reduce the glycaemic load, and limit or omit the tempura component to avoid additional refined carbohydrates and high-GI batter.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Salmon provides cardioprotective omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); however, sodium at 300 mg/100 g (1800 mg per serving) and optional deep-fried tempura component are cardiovascular risk factors
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease benefit from the omega-3 content of the salt-grilled salmon but should request reduced sodium preparation (less miso, soy sauce, and salt) and omit the tempura component to minimise saturated fat and sodium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Salmon is a rich source of anti-inflammatory omega-3 fatty acids (EPA/DHA); however, optional deep-fried tempura generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should consume this set meal without the tempura component and request reduced miso and soy sauce to limit sodium-driven tissue swelling.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Shioyaki Grilled Salmon Set Meal (塩焼きサーモン定食, Shioyaki Sāmon Teishoku; Chinese: 盐烤三文鱼套餐; Malay: Set Makan Salmon Panggang Shioyaki) is a traditional Japanese teishoku (set meal) served widely in Japanese restaurants in Singapore and Japan. The centrepiece is a salmon fillet seasoned only with salt and grilled over direct heat (shioyaki method), producing a lightly charred, crispy skin with moist, flaky flesh and a clean, savoury flavour. The set is typically served with a bowl of steamed white rice (~200 g), a bowl of miso soup made from miso paste, dashi stock, silken tofu, and wakame seaweed, and optional side dishes such as tempura (deep-fried prawns or vegetables in a light batter), pickled vegetables (tsukemono), or a small salad. The total set meal weighs approximately 600 g. Per 100 g, the set provides approximately 125 kcal, 9.2 g protein, 5.8 g fat (1.3 g saturated), 9.2 g carbohydrate, 0.8 g sugar, 0.7 g dietary fibre, and 300 mg sodium. The salmon component is one of the richest dietary sources of long-chain omega-3 fatty acids (EPA and DHA), which are cardioprotective and anti-inflammatory. Clinically, the primary concern is sodium: miso paste, dashi, soy sauce, and salt-grilling collectively contribute approximately 1800 mg sodium per full set, approaching the WHO daily limit in a single meal, making this dish a high-sodium concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The white rice component carries a medium glycaemic index (GI ~64) and contributes approximately 55 g available carbohydrate per serving (GL ~35), which is moderate-to-high for individuals managing type 2 diabetes. Requesting brown rice, a smaller rice portion, and omission of tempura substantially improves the nutritional profile for metabolic and cardiovascular health.

confidence_reason: No single approved-source entry exists for a composite shioyaki salmon teishoku set meal. Nutrient values are estimated by weighting USDA FoodData Central component entries (salmon cooked dry heat, cooked white rice, miso soup components) according to the estimated set meal composition (150 g salmon, 200 g rice, 200 g miso soup, 50 g sides). The input record's per-100g values are broadly consistent with this weighted composite and are retained. GI is estimated from the white rice component using the University of Sydney GI Database. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite estimation basis.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite set meal is not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database; individual salmon component fatty acid data exists in USDA FDC (FDC ID 175168) but cannot be reliably apportioned to the composite per-100g figure without introducing additional estimation error. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: micronutrient data for this composite set meal is not available as a single validated entry in any approved source; component-level data exists in USDA FDC but reliable composite apportionment is not possible within the scope of this record. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100g (trace level from salmon fat); this is a naturally occurring trace amount, not industrially produced trans fat. The input source listed (foodo.sg) is not an approved primary nutrient source under Project Veritas v2 source policy; nutrient values have been re-anchored to USDA FoodData Central as the approved source. The optional tempura component introduces variability in fat, carbohydrate, and sodium values; the per-100g figures represent the full set meal as typically served including tempura. Halal status is marked false as miso paste and dashi may contain non-halal ingredients depending on preparation; consumers should verify with the specific restaurant. This dish is served in Singapore and is of Japanese cuisine origin; multilingual names are provided in Japanese (mandatory), Chinese simplified and traditional, Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to support Singapore-context retrieval. Ontology match types corrected to 'closest_parent' for both FoodOn and FoodEx2 per allowed values ['closest_parent', 'exact', 'none'].
